## FINAL YEAR PROJECT (Detailed)
**Professional Social Network for Academic Research Recommendation**
**Duration:** Jul 2018 - Jan 2020 (2 semesters) | **Team:** Ali & Usman
**GitHub:** github.com/FYP-Ali-Usman

### Problem
• Existing systems (Google Scholar, ResearchGate) lack network visualization
• Recommendations based only on citations, not relationships
• No social network analysis

### Solution
• Scrape author/publication data dynamically
• Apply centrality algorithms (degree, closeness, betweenness, eigenvector)
• Interactive D3.js network visualization
• Network-based recommendations
• Forum system for discussion

### Tech Stack
• **Frontend:** Angular 8, TypeScript, D3.js v5
• **Backend:** Django, Python
• **Database:** MongoDB
• **Scraping:** Selenium, BeautifulSoup
• **Graph Analysis:** NetworkX

### Six Major Modules

**1. Data Crawler**
• University websites → Faculty names → Microsoft Academia → Author profiles
• Intelligent parsing: Titles, designations, publications
• Handled 500+ authors, 5,000+ publications
• GitHub module: github.com/AliHafeez337/FYP-Extracting-author-names-from-faculty-web-pages

**2. Data Visualization**
• D3.js force-directed graph
• Interactive: Hover, click, drag, zoom, filter
• Node size based on centrality
• Edge thickness based on collaboration count
• GitHub projects:
  - github.com/AliHafeez337/FYP-D3_Js_v4.0-Network_visualization
  - github.com/AliHafeez337/NetworkVisualization-d3v4
  - github.com/AliHafeez337/D3-network-visualization-in-Angular-8
  - github.com/mkobar/is-angular8-d3-graph-example2 (contributed to)

**3. Social Network Analysis**
• Graph construction from collaborations
• **Four Centrality Algorithms:**
  - Degree: Direct collaborators count
  - Closeness: Average distance to all others (BFS)
  - Betweenness: Frequency on shortest paths (Brandes')
  - Eigenvector: Importance of connections (linear algebra)
• Performance: <10 seconds for 1000-node graphs

**4. Search & Filter**
• Multi-criteria search: Author, publication, organization, interest
• Hybrid: Database cache + live crawling
• Background tasks with Celery
• Real-time progress via WebSocket
• Advanced filtering options

**5. Recommendations**
• Item-item collaborative filtering
• Interest-based matching
• Network-based suggestions
• Cosine similarity algorithm

**6. Forum System**
• Hierarchical comments/replies
• Upvote/downvote system
• User reputation tracking
• Spam prevention

### Achievements
• 15,000 lines of code (Frontend: 6K, Backend: 5K, Algorithms: 2K)
• 500+ authors, 5,000+ publications, 10,000+ collaborations
• 67-page final report
• All 4 centrality algorithms working
• Interactive visualization for large graphs

### Impact
• Immediate portfolio piece
• Demonstrated: Full-stack, algorithms, research
• Interview talking point
• Foundation for MS research, professional work

### Problem Statement
Existing academic search systems (Google Scholar, ResearchGate, Microsoft Academia) have limitations:
- No interactive network visualization
- Recommendations based solely on citations
- No social network analysis of collaborations
- Static representations of research communities

### Our Solution
Build a system that:
- Scrapes author and publication data dynamically
- Applies social network analysis (centrality algorithms)
- Provides interactive network visualization
- Offers network-based recommendations
- Enables community discussion via forum

### Technology Stack

- **Backend:** Django 2.2, Python 3.7
- **Database:** MongoDB 4.0
- **Web Scraping:** Selenium WebDriver, BeautifulSoup4
- **Graph Analysis:** NetworkX 2.3
- **Deployment:** Heroku (backend), Firebase Hosting (frontend)

### Algorithm 1: Cleaning Scraped Data

**Purpose:** Remove navigation elements, scripts, and invalid text

**Pseudocode:**
```python
def clean_scraped_text(page_source):
    # Extract all text blocks
    texts = extract_all_text(page_source)
    
    cleaned = []
    for text in texts:
        # Remove if contains path separators
        if '/' in text or '\\' in text:
            continue
        
        # Remove if contains script tags
        if '<script>' in text or '</script>' in text:
            continue
        
        # Remove very short or very long text
        if len(text) < 3 or len(text) > 200:
            continue
        
        cleaned.append(text)
    
    return cleaned
```

### Algorithm 3: Crawling Microsoft Academia

**Selenium WebDriver Implementation:**

```python
from selenium import webdriver
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup
import time

def search_author_on_academia(author_name, university):
    # Setup
    driver = webdriver.Chrome()
    search_query = f"{author_name} {university}"
    url = f"https://academic.microsoft.com/search?q={search_query}"
    
    try:
        # Load page
        driver.get(url)
        time.sleep(3)  # Wait for JavaScript
        
        # Parse with BeautifulSoup
        soup = BeautifulSoup(driver.page_source, 'html.parser')
        
        # Find author link
        author_element = soup.find('a', class_='author-link')
        if author_element:
            return author_element['href']
        
        return None
    
    finally:
        driver.quit()
```

**Complete Data Extraction:**

```python
def scrape_author_complete(author_url):
    soup = get_page_soup(author_url)
    
    author_data = {
        'name': extract_name(soup),
        'affiliation': extract_affiliation(soup),
        'research_interests': extract_interests(soup),
        'total_papers': extract_paper_count(soup),
        'total_citations': extract_citation_count(soup),
        'h_index': extract_h_index(soup),
        'papers': [],
        'coauthors': []
    }
    
    # Pagination for papers
    page = 1
    max_pages = calculate_pages(author_data['total_papers'], papers_per_page=10)
    
    while page <= max_pages:
        papers_on_page = extract_papers_from_page(soup)
        
        for paper in papers_on_page:
            paper_data = {
                'title': paper['title'],
                'year': paper['year'],
                'venue': paper['venue'],
                'citations': paper['citations'],
                'coauthors': extract_coauthors_from_paper(paper)
            }
            author_data['papers'].append(paper_data)
            
            # Collect unique co-authors
            for coauthor in paper_data['coauthors']:
                if coauthor not in author_data['coauthors']:
                    author_data['coauthors'].append(coauthor)
        
        # Next page
        page += 1
        soup = get_page_soup(f"{author_url}?page={page}")
    
    return author_data
```

### Graph Construction

**NetworkX Implementation:**

```python
import networkx as nx

def build_collaboration_graph():
    G = nx.Graph()
    
    # Add nodes (authors)
    authors = db.authors.find()
    for author in authors:
        G.add_node(author['_id'], 
                   name=author['name'],
                   affiliation=author['affiliation'])
    
    # Add edges (collaborations)
    from collections import defaultdict
    collab_weight = defaultdict(int)
    
    papers = db.publications.find()
    for paper in papers:
        coauthors = paper['authors']
        for i in range(len(coauthors)):
            for j in range(i+1, len(coauthors)):
                edge = tuple(sorted([coauthors[i], coauthors[j]]))
                collab_weight[edge] += 1
    
    for (a1, a2), weight in collab_weight.items():
        G.add_edge(a1, a2, weight=weight)
    
    return G
```

```

**3. Betweenness Centrality:**

```

**4. Eigenvector Centrality:**
```python
try:
    eigenvector_cent = nx.eigenvector_centrality(G, weight='weight', max_iter=1000)
except nx.PowerIterationFailedConvergence:
    # Fallback to NumPy
    import numpy as np
    from scipy.sparse.linalg import eigs
    
    adj = nx.to_scipy_sparse_matrix(G, weight='weight')
    eigenvalues, eigenvectors = eigs(adj.asfptype(), k=1, which='LR')
    eigenvector = eigenvectors[:, 0].real
    eigenvector = eigenvector / eigenvector.sum()
    
    eigenvector_cent = dict(zip(G.nodes(), eigenvector))
```

**Combined Calculation:**
```python
def compute_all_centralities(G):
    return {
        node: {
            'degree': nx.degree_centrality(G)[node],
            'closeness': nx.closeness_centrality(G)[node],
            'betweenness': nx.betweenness_centrality(G, weight='weight')[node],
            'eigenvector': nx.eigenvector_centrality(G, weight='weight')[node]
        }
        for node in G.nodes()
    }
```

### Item-Item Collaborative Filtering

```python
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
import numpy as np

class RecommendationEngine:
    def __init__(self):
        self.user_paper_matrix = None
        self.similarity_matrix = None
        self.paper_ids = []
    
    def build_matrix(self):
        users = list(db.users.find())
        papers = list(db.publications.find())
        
        self.paper_ids = [p['_id'] for p in papers]
        matrix = np.zeros((len(users), len(papers)))
        
        # Fill based on interactions
        # Views = 1, Bookmarks = 2, Comments = 3
        user_idx = {u['_id']: i for i, u in enumerate(users)}
        paper_idx = {p['_id']: i for i, p in enumerate(papers)}
        
        for view in db.views.find():
            matrix[user_idx[view['user_id']], paper_idx[view['paper_id']]] += 1
        
        for bookmark in db.bookmarks.find():
            matrix[user_idx[bookmark['user_id']], paper_idx[bookmark['paper_id']]] += 2
        
        for comment in db.comments.find():
            matrix[user_idx[comment['user_id']], paper_idx[comment['paper_id']]] += 3
        
        self.user_paper_matrix = matrix
    
    def calc_similarities(self):
        # Transpose: papers × users
        paper_vectors = self.user_paper_matrix.T
        self.similarity_matrix = cosine_similarity(paper_vectors)
    
    def recommend(self, paper_id, k=10):
        if paper_id not in self.paper_ids:
            return []
        
        idx = self.paper_ids.index(paper_id)
        similarities = self.similarity_matrix[idx]
        
        # Sort and exclude self
        similar_idx = np.argsort(similarities)[::-1][1:k+1]
        
        return [(self.paper_ids[i], similarities[i]) for i in similar_idx]
```

### Unit Tests

```python
import unittest
import networkx as nx

class TestCentrality(unittest.TestCase):
    def setUp(self):
        self.G = nx.Graph()
        self.G.add_edges_from([('A','B'), ('A','C'), ('B','D'), ('C','D')])
    
    def test_degree_centrality(self):
        cent = nx.degree_centrality(self.G)
        self.assertAlmostEqual(cent['A'], 2/3)
        self.assertAlmostEqual(cent['B'], 2/3)
    
    def test_betweenness(self):
        cent = nx.betweenness_centrality(self.G)
        self.assertEqual(cent['A'], 0)  # Endpoint
        self.assertGreater(cent['B'], 0)  # On paths

chunks.text:
## ABSTRACT

LoRaWAN is an IoT networking protocol gaining significant industry attention. Edge devices communicate through untrusted Gateways to a Network Server. These devices run on single charge for 10+ years.

**What We Did:**
1. Identified novel DoS attack: Malicious gateway modifies downlink packets causing MIC failures
2. Demonstrated complete DoS (100%) with single malicious gateway
3. Proposed mitigation: Multi-path downlink selection (random from top 3 gateways)
4. Implemented in NS-3 simulator (C++ modifications)
5. Tested scenarios: 1, 2, 10 gateways
6. Results: 70-85% communication recovery under attack

**Key Finding:** LoRaWAN uses multi-path for uplink (resilient) but single-path for downlink (vulnerable). Our mitigation adds redundancy to downlink.

---

chunks.text:
## 1. LORAWAN BACKGROUND

**LoRa Technology:**
- Long Range wireless modulation (up to 15km)
- Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)
- Ultra-low power (10+ year battery life)
- Unlicensed ISM bands (868 MHz in EU)

**LoRaWAN Protocol:**
- Network layer on top of LoRa
- Star-of-stars topology
- Components: End Devices (ED) → Gateways (GW) → Network Server (NS)
- **Gateways are untrusted** in security model

**Communication:**
- **Uplink:** ED broadcasts → All GWs receive → All forward to NS → NS accepts first valid
- **Downlink:** NS selects BEST gateway → Single GW sends → ED receives in RX1/RX2 window
- **Key Asymmetry:** Uplink = multi-path, Downlink = single-path

**Security:**
- AES-128 encryption
- Message Integrity Code (MIC) - 4 bytes, AES-CMAC
- MIC calculated over all fields using session key
- Any modification → MIC mismatch → Packet rejected

---

## 2. LITERATURE REVIEW SUMMARY

Reviewed 16+ papers on LoRaWAN security:

**Key Management:** Han & Wang (root key updates), Sanchez-Iborra (EDHOC), Chen (Rabbit cipher)

**Attack Mitigation:** Noura (MITM, Replay, Jamming countermeasures), Franz (physical attacks, DoS, blockchain authentication)

**Network Performance:** Qian (SDN + ARIMA for path selection), Wang (ReLoRaWAN multi-GW recovery), Tian (LoRaHop mesh)

**Gateway Security:** Mohamed (certificate authentication reducing illegitimate packets 66.67%)

**Gap Identified:**
- Most work on uplink attacks or key management
- **No study on downlink single-path vulnerability**
- **No MIC failure attack via malicious gateway**
- **No security-focused multi-path downlink**

chunks.text:
## 3. NOVEL ATTACK: DOWNLINK MIC FAILURE

**Attack Mechanism:**
- Malicious gateway modifies MAC Header (MHDR) in downlink packet
- Example: Change MType from "Confirmed Data Down" (101) to "Unconfirmed Data Down" (011)
- MIC was calculated with original MType
- Modified packet has different MType
- ED receives → Calculates MIC → Mismatch → Rejects ACK

**Why It Works:**
- NS selects **only ONE gateway** for downlink (best RSSI)
- If that gateway is malicious → 100% DoS
- No alternative path for ED to receive ACK
- ED retransmits → Drains battery → Never succeeds

**Two Scenarios Explored:**

**Case 1: Uplink MIC Failure (Ineffective)**
- Malicious GW modifies uplink before forwarding to NS
- **Problem:** Multiple GWs forward uplink → NS receives unmodified copies from others
- Attack fails unless ALL GWs malicious (unrealistic)

**Case 2: Downlink MIC Failure (CRITICAL)**
- Malicious GW modifies downlink before transmitting to ED
- **Problem:** Only ONE GW used for downlink → No alternatives
- **Result:** Complete DoS, 100% effective if malicious GW selected as best

**Attack Flow:**
```
ED sends uplink → Multiple GWs receive → NS selects best GW (malicious) 
→ NS sends ACK to malicious GW → GW modifies MType → ED receives 
→ MIC fails → ACK rejected → ED retransmits (battery drain) → Cycle repeats
```

**Attacker Requirements:**
- Deploy malicious gateway in target area
- Position to have best RSSI (highest signal)
- No need to break encryption or know session keys
- Trivial packet modification

---

chunks.text:
## 4. PROPOSED MITIGATION

**Core Idea:** Apply uplink's multi-path redundancy to downlink

**Instead of:**
- NS always selects best gateway (highest RSSI)
- Single point of failure

**Our Approach:**
- NS maintains top 3 gateways (by RSSI)
- Randomly selects from top 3 for each downlink
- Even if best GW malicious, 66.7% chance of selecting legitimate GW

**Algorithm:**
1. After each uplink: NS sorts GWs by RSSI
2. NS stores top 3 GWs for that device
3. When downlink needed: Random selection from top 3
4. Selected GW sends downlink

**Why It Works:**
- Probabilistic redundancy
- With 3 candidates (1 malicious, 2 legitimate):
  - P(success in 1 attempt) = 2/3 = 66.7%
  - P(success in 2 attempts) = 88.9%
  - P(success in 3 attempts) = 96.3%
- ED retransmits → Different GW selected → Eventually succeeds

**Trade-offs:**
- ✅ Significant DoS resistance
- ✅ Battery life preserved (vs constant retransmissions)
- ✅ Simple (only NS modification, no protocol changes)
- ❌ Requires ≥2 gateways (doesn't work with 1 GW)
- ❌ 2nd/3rd GW may be farther → potential packet loss
- ❌ Slight performance decrease vs baseline (if no attack)

---

## 5. IMPLEMENTATION IN NS-3

**Network Simulator 3 (NS-3):**
- Open-source discrete-event network simulator
- C++ implementation
- Extended lorawan-module with custom code

**Modifications Made:**
1. **Malicious Gateway Class:** Intercepts downlink, modifies MType field
2. **Network Server Enhancement:** Random selection from top N gateways (configurable)
3. **Metrics Collection:** Delivery ratio, average delay, throughput, GW selection distribution

**Simulation Topology:**
- 1 End Device at ground level (0, 0, 0)
- 1, 2, or 10 Gateways at 100m altitude, random horizontal positions within 1000km
- 1 Network Server (virtual)

**Scenarios Tested:**
- **1-GW:** Baseline, Attack, Mitigation (9 total: 3 configs × 1 run)
- **2-GW:** Baseline, Attack, Mitigation (30 total: 3 configs × 10 runs for statistics)
- **10-GW:** Baseline, Attack, Mitigation (30 total: 3 configs × 10 runs)

**Parameters:**
- Simulation time: 60 minutes (3600 seconds)
- Uplink interval: 1.5 seconds
- LoRa: SF7, 868 MHz, 125 kHz BW, 14 dBm TX power
- Metrics: Averaged over 10 runs per scenario (except 1-GW)

chunks.text:
### Key Findings

**1-Gateway Results:**
- **Attack:** Complete DoS (0% delivery ratio)
- **Mitigation:** Ineffective (still 0%) - needs ≥2 GWs for diversity
- **Conclusion:** Single GW deployment remains fully vulnerable

**2-Gateway Results:**
- **Attack:** Partial DoS (32.5% delivery) - sometimes non-malicious GW selected by chance
- **Mitigation:** 73.2% delivery - more than doubles success rate!
- **Delay Improved:** 0.98s vs 1.09s baseline (load balancing effect)
- **Conclusion:** Mitigation highly effective with just 2 GWs

**10-Gateway Results:**
- **Attack:** Moderate DoS (54.3%) - more alternatives reduce impact
- **Mitigation:** 81.7% delivery - excellent recovery
- **Delay Best:** 0.92s - best of all scenarios (load balancing + path diversity)
- **Conclusion:** Dense deployments maximize mitigation effectiveness

**Why Mitigation Has Lower Delay Than Baseline:**
- Load balancing: Distributes traffic across multiple GWs, reduces congestion
- Path diversity: Sometimes finds faster path than "best" GW
- Random selection explores alternatives that baseline ignores

**Why Delivery Ratio Decreases Slightly Over Time (in 10-GW mitigation):**
- Distance factor: Top 3 GWs may be far from ED (random placement)
- Statistical variance accumulates over 2400 transmissions
- Trade-off: Security gain vs slight performance cost
- Still excellent: 81.7% vs 0-50% under attack

---

## 7. COMPARISONS

**Mitigation vs Attack:**
| Scenario | Attack | Mitigation | Improvement |
|----------|--------|------------|-------------|
| 1-GW | 0% | 0% | N/A |
| 2-GW | 32.5% | 73.2% | +125% |
| 10-GW | 54.3% | 81.7% | +50% |

**Mitigation vs Baseline:**
- Delivery ratio: -14% (acceptable security trade-off)
- Delay: -16% (improved! Load balancing benefit)
- Throughput: -14% (follows delivery ratio)

**Cost-Benefit:**
- **Cost:** Minimal (NS software change only)
- **Benefit:** 50-125% improvement in delivery ratio under attack
- **ROI:** Very high, especially for security-critical deployments

## 8. DISCUSSION

**Why Mitigation Works:**
- Leverages path diversity (same principle as uplink resilience)
- Probabilistic redundancy through random selection
- Multiple attempts eventually succeed

**Limitations:**
- Doesn't work with 1 GW (requires ≥2)
- Multiple malicious GWs reduce effectiveness
- Distance penalty (farther GWs → weaker signal)
- Doesn't detect malicious GW, only mitigates

**Real-World Deployment:**
- **Urban:** 5-10 GWs per area → Highly effective
- **Suburban:** 2-5 GWs → Moderately effective
- **Rural:** 1 GW → Ineffective, need more GWs

**Recommendations:**
- Deploy ≥3 gateways per coverage area
- Enable mitigation by default in Network Server
- Monitor delivery ratios for anomaly detection
- Combine with gateway authentication (future work)

## 10. CONCLUSIONS

**Research Summary:**
1. ✅ **Identified vulnerability:** LoRaWAN downlink single-path creates DoS opportunity
2. ✅ **Demonstrated attack:** MIC failure via malicious gateway (100% DoS with 1 GW)
3. ✅ **Proposed mitigation:** Multi-downlink path selection (random from top 3)
4. ✅ **Validated effectiveness:** 70-85% communication recovery under attack
5. ✅ **Open-source:** Code available on GitHub for community

**Key Contributions:**
- **Novel attack discovery** (first to identify downlink vulnerability)
- **Practical mitigation** (simple, deployable, no protocol changes)
- **Comprehensive evaluation** (NS-3 simulations across multiple scenarios)

**Impact:**
- **Network Operators:** Deploy ≥3 GWs, enable mitigation
- **Developers:** Design for 70-80% reliability
- **Protocol Designers:** Consider multi-path downlink in future standards
- **Security Community:** Path diversity as security primitive

**Personal Growth:**
- Deep LoRaWAN protocol expertise
- NS-3 simulation skills (C++)
- Research methodology (literature review → gap → solution → validation)
- Academic writing (50-page thesis in LaTeX)
- Presentation and defense

**Final Thought:** Security is about architectural decisions. LoRaWAN's single-path downlink was an efficiency choice that created vulnerability. Simple addition of redundancy provides significant security improvement.

  },
  trafficEnabled: true,
  myLocationEnabled: true,
)

// Real-time traffic updates
void startTrafficUpdates() {
  Timer.periodic(Duration(seconds: 30), (timer) async {
    final trafficData = await grpcClient.getTrafficData();
    updateMapWithTrafficData(trafficData);
  });
}
```

#### gRPC Integration

### DIGITAL TWIN SIMULATOR (Web Application)

**Purpose:** Simulate Airvi concept in controlled environment using traffic simulation

#### Technical Architecture

**Frontend (Flutter Web):**
• Same Flutter codebase as mobile (with platform-specific adjustments)
• Rendered to web (HTML/CSS/JavaScript via Dart compiler)
• Canvas-based rendering for performance

**Backend:**
• **Python microservices:** Traffic simulation control
• **SUMO (Simulation of Urban MObility):** Traffic simulator
• **OSM (OpenStreetMap) data:** Real-world road networks
• **gRPC:** Communication protocol

**Data Pipeline:**
```
OSM Maps
    ↓ (preprocessing)
SUMO Network Files
    ↓
SUMO Simulation Engine
    ↓ (real-time data via TraCI)
Python Control Layer
    ↓ (gRPC)
Flutter Web App
```

---

#### SUMO Integration

**What is SUMO?**
• Open-source traffic simulation software
• Microscopic simulation (individual vehicles)
• Command-line driven
• Supports large-scale scenarios

**SUMO Components Used:**
• **netconvert:** Convert OSM to SUMO network
• **polyconvert:** Convert additional map features
• **randomTrips.py:** Generate vehicle routes
• **SUMO-GUI:** Graphical viewer (for development)
• **sumo:** Simulation engine (for production)
• **TraCI:** Traffic Control Interface (Python API)

**Workflow:**
1. **Map Preparation:**
```bash

# Generate random trips
randomTrips.py -n city.net.xml -r routes.rou.xml -e 3600 --fringe-factor 10
```

3. **Simulation Configuration:**
```xml
<configuration>
    <input>
        <net-file value="city.net.xml"/>
        <route-files value="routes.rou.xml"/>
    </input>
    <time>
        <begin value="0"/>
        <end value="3600"/>
    </time>
</configuration>
```

4. **Run with TraCI Control:**
```python
import traci

# Start SUMO
traci.start(["sumo", "-c", "city.sumocfg", "--remote-port", "8813"])

# Spatial grid for fast neighbor lookup class SpatialGrid: def __init__(self, cell_size=100): self.cell_size = cell_size self.grid = defaultdict(list) def insert(self, vehicle): cell = self.get_cell(vehicle.position) self.grid[cell].append(vehicle) def get_nearby(self, position, radius): nearby_vehicles = [] for dx in [-1, 0, 1]: for dy in [-1, 0, 1]: cell = self.get_cell(position, dx, dy) nearby_vehicles.extend(self.grid.get(cell, [])) return nearby_vehicles ``` --- #### Key Learnings from Project **Flutter:** • First major Flutter project - steep learning curve but worthwhile • Flutter Web performance good for this use case • Platform-specific code when needed (mobile vs web) • Widget composition pattern very powerful **gRPC:** • More efficient than REST for real-time data • Envoy proxy essential for web/mobile clients • Protocol Buffers require upfront schema design • Streaming gRPC excellent for real-time updates **SUMO:** • Powerful simulation tool with learning curve • TraCI API allows programmatic control • Performance bottleneck can be data transmission (thousands of vehicles) • Real-world maps need cleanup before use **System Integration:** • Multiple components (Flutter, Python, SUMO, Envoy) require careful orchestration • Containerization (Docker) helps with deployment • Monitoring and logging critical for debugging --- **For complete Flowtack, MBP CRM, and other projects, see the separate continuation artifact due to length constraints.**

PTA needs to process WhatsApp numbers in batches, submit them to Meta for blocking verification, and track detailed status for each number through the full lifecycle (upload → approval → Meta processing → final blocked / not blocked outcome).

chunks.text:
### Core Workflow

1. **User** uploads WhatsApp numbers in **batches** (bulk upload)
2. **Approver** reviews and **approves** batches before submission to Meta
3. Approved batches are **sent to Meta**
4. **Meta** responds with per-number status (blocked or not, and related states)
5. System tracks **many progress statuses** through the pipeline until final outcome
6. **Admin** oversees users, permissions, and full application control

meta.file: PTA - WhatsApp Blocking Platform - Detailed Documentation.md

### User Roles (RBAC)

| Role | Responsibilities |
|------|------------------|
| **User** | Upload WhatsApp number batches; view status of own submissions |
| **Approver** | Review uploaded batches; approve or reject before Meta submission |
| **Admin** | Manage all users; full application configuration and oversight |
